AoE Workshop Questions, Week 3





Module 5: Value Newsletters (part 2)

Rich Porter

1) How do you decide which VNL's to repurpose for your website (how do you think about that) and have you ever republished an entire series of VNL's to your site?



Example series republished (five-part series for TDB republished as a single long-form article): https://tinylittlebusinesses.com/the-durable-business-series-part-1/

Francis Waldecker

One thing I've always struggled with is what to give away for free in the emails I write. I tend to want to share/educate a bit too much.

Do you have any good frameworks or questions I can ask myself to make sure I'm sharing valuable insights but not going to the point where they feel like they no longer need the course/coaching anymore

Module 6: Bridge Emails

Karma Lodro Seng

So I am still slightly unclear on the differences between a "Soft Bridge" and "Hard Bridge" emails are. Is a soft bridge just you telling a person that the next email is coming (with a possible promotion), and a Hard Bridge email one where they have to opt-in to the email sequence?



In the Bridge Email Module, do you think you can break down the "Andre's Story Path" image?





(Image URL.)



Rich Porter

About the bridge email, you wrote:

this is the most effective framework to identify the essential needs of an audience and match those needs precisely to the product or service you offer

2) Is there a feedback loop in here to really nail identification and matching of their needs, or do you mean that by raising or not raising their hand only?

3) Do you use the results of these bridge emails (% of raised hands, replies, comments) to influence your paid ads (to build better customers)?

4) Do you ever ask people who don't raise their hand after a bridge why they didn't in the following VNL, or is that too granular (or too big a pain in the butt lol)?

5) The bridge emails are a big inflection point and I can't tell if I'm looking for too much from them (because the long timeline for most buyers makes immediate feedback less useful) or not enough (because we want to stack as many 7-10's and this would seem like a source of customer modeling). Is any of that right?





Non-Module Specific Questions

Ricardo Clarke

In the case that you have a premium or high ticket offer and are running ads to a typical application funnel, how could that non linear "world" look? 

Typically a prospect goes from Ad > Landing Page > VSL > Application > Call. 

I guess it's like blurring the lines of SOI and AoE. The process after they opt-in to see the VSL and don't fill out an applicant can 100% be ARMified with a serialized narrative. I am wandering if there is a better upstream approach especially when spending a lot on Ads for a high ticket offer.

The backend approach still includes a nurture sequence which then leads into a newsletter and a subtle call to action to fill out an application but again wondering if there is a better approach upstream for this specific type of situation.

Anthony Zhang

What are your thoughts on the "strategy" of running front-end & backend offers? As you know it very well, with the typical funnel approach, marketers would try to breakeven or even lose some money initially to get a customer using a low-cost offer and then sell a more expensive product(s) later as the backend offer. Since funnel is no longer something we need to worry about, should this frontend-backend "strategy" be thrown out of the window as well?

If you run a marketing agency & charge clients monthly retainers and face-to-face communication plays a critically important role in winning/keeping clients, would you still implement everything mentioned in AoE? I think VNL and COS will still be highly relevant and super useful but not sure about the other components…could you please shed some light on this?



Mary Grisolia

I've been writing emails since several years back now, and sometimes I've wrote what I'd consider to be really good ones (I bought ARM 1.0 so I have a nice background!).

I'm letting ideas for RBSs and SPPs start to form in my mind, and it occurred to me that I could go to my autoresponder service and recover those old, good emails to see if I could use them somehow. But I'm afraid that, by doing so, I'd be mutilating my creativity and writing process and somehow messing with progress as a writer.

Do you think it can be a good idea to re-use old emails? Or it would be better to start off from scratch?



Andy McDuff

In the overall strategy, would you be able to provide an example or provide some ideas on how one could apply AoE to local or even regional businesses? For example, a carpet fitting or boiler installer business. In service-based businesses, I can see how I could apply AoE (e.g. accounting services) but finding it somewhat harder to figure out something for local businesses that actually provide some kind of service in people's homes.

Jen Kuo

My question is about your Re Engagement Email Series for list pruning.

You mentioned that your series is 4 emails long, giving unengaged subscribers 4 opportunities to raise their hand so they don't get deleted off your list.

What does the Re Engagement Email Series look like?

Do they all have the same message repeated 4 times across the 4 emails? What frequency are they sent out?

I don't remember seeing a real example of your Re Engagement Email Series since I've been a subscriber for a long time.

It would be nice to see an example to model as best practice.
